title: Haloragis micrantha
intro: Haloragis micrantha, also known as small dodder or coastal dodder, is a shrub-like plant native to Australia and New Zealand, characterized by its wiry stems and small white or yellow flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in the treatment of respiratory issues and skin conditions. Bioactive constituents of Haloragis micrantha include alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Haloragis micrantha is often prepared as a tea or tincture, and is also used in ointments and creams to treat skin conditions such as eczema and acne.
taxonomy: Haloragis micrantha is a species of flowering plant in the family Haloragaceae, commonly known as the water-dotted tangle or water daisy. This plant is native to Australia and is often found in wetlands and aquatic environments. It is a type of aquatic plant that grows in dense clusters and has small, white or yellow flowers. As a member of the Haloragaceae family, Haloragis micrantha is closely related to other plants such as the watermilfoil and the water parsley. The plant's classification is also reflected in its botanical name, which indicates its genus and species within the Haloragaceae family.
uses: Haloragis micrantha, also known as New Zealand dodder, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine. The plant's leaves and stems are said to have anti-inflammatory properties and are used to treat various ailments, including fever and skin conditions.
